text: The surgical team places ports, including an 11mm assistant port, to facilitate robot docking for the duodenal jejunostomy procedure.

The surgeon creates a roolim in the supracholic compartment, ensuring it lays without tension. The mesentery is divided close to the bowel, and the gap is widened using a bowel grasper to facilitate the placement of a linear cutting stapler. The stapler is used to divide the bowel and confirm the orientation of the root. Scissors with electrocautery are employed to create enterotomies on both sides, followed by another firing of the linear cutting stapler to establish the jejuno-jejunostomy.
The surgeon closes the enterotomy of the common limb using a running IV or V-lock stitch, followed by a seromuscular III or vicryl stitch.

The surgeon begins dissection in the supracolic compartment, revealing inflammation and adhesions near the gallbladder due to recurrent pancreatitis or duodenal obstruction. The hepatic flexure is widely mobilized to facilitate later Kocherization, achieved laparoscopically in this case.

The hepatic flexure is fully mobilized, revealing the duodenum. The annular pancreas is visible, causing circumferential obstruction of the duodenum. The proximal duodenum appears dilated and relaxed due to increased mobilization.

The surgeon completes the mobilization and corporalization of the duodenum, revealing the annular pancreas causing obstruction. The proximal duodenum, which sits without tension, is approximated to the previously created roux limb through a defect in the mesocolon within the supracolic compartment.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to create a two-layer anastomosis, visible as a posterior seromuscular row constructed with three ovicle stitches. The jejunum is positioned in the supracolic compartment, forming an isoperistaltic joint.

The surgeon uses robotic hock cautery to create enterotomies on both the duodenum and jejunum limbs. The posterior cuff of tissue is prepared for stitching the back wall of the anastomosis, ensuring the enterotomies are even while considering the tendency of the dilated duodenum and jejunum to expand under tension.

The surgeon uses forovacral stitches to create a continuous anastomosis, starting with the back wall and ensuring the mucosa is grabbed with each stitch. The anterior wall is then stitched, with the mucosa inverted using a canal technique. The robotic system assists in this process.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis by adding a four ovicral stitch for an additional seromuscular layer, forming a two-layer side-to-side functional end-to-end duodenal jejunostomy in the supracolic compartment.
